input_row.title: Ensure stored access policies (SAP) are used when generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens
input_row.description: Use stored access policies (SAP) when generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens in Azure to centrally manage permissions, expiration, and revocation settings for resource access. Stored access policies can be applied to blob containers, file shares, queues, and tables.
input_row.rationale: Stored access policies provide centralized control over SAS token access, allowing administrators to update permissions or revoke access. This approach strengthens security by reducing the risk of unauthorized access to storage resources. Impact: There is no cost for creating stored access policies, however there is some administrative overhead involved in managing these policies.
input_row.audit: It is not currently possible to retrieve a list of all generated SAS tokens to check if they were associated with a SAP during creation. An SAS token that has been created with a SAP will contain an si parameter that references the stored access policy identifier associated with the SAS, e.g. si=<stored-access-policy-identifier>. The si parameter will be absent from an SAS token created without a SAP.
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal To create a SAP for a queue: 1. Go to Storage accounts. 2. Click on a storage account. 3. Under Data storage, click Queues. 4. Click the name of a queue. 5. Under Settings, click Access policy. 6. Under Stored access policies, click + Add policy. 7. Enter an Identifier. 8. From the Permissions drop-down, select appropriate permissions for the policy. 9. Set an appropriate Start time for the policy. 10. Set an appropriate Expiry time for the policy. 11. Click OK. 12. Click Save. 13. Repeat steps 1-12 as needed to create SAP. When generating SAS, select a SAP from the Stored access policy drop-down. If SAS have been created without a SAP, the SAS can be revoked by regenerating the storage account access keys: Note: Regenerating access keys can affect any applications or Azure services that are dependent on the storage account key. 1. Go to Storage accounts. 2. Click on a storage account. 3. Under Security + networking, click Access keys. 4. Next to each key, click Rotate key. 5. Click Yes to confirm. 6. Repeat steps 1-5 as needed to revoke SAS. Default Value: By default, stored access policies are not associated with SAS. To use a stored access policy, it must be explicitly created and linked to the SAS at the time of creation. References: 1. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-sas- overview#best-practices-when-using-sas 2. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/storageservices/define-stored-access- policy Additional Information: This recommendation is based on the recommendation Ensure stored access policies (SAP) are used when generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens, from the Common Reference Recommendations > Secrets and Keys > Shared Access Signatures section.
input_row.impact: There is no cost for creating stored access policies, however there is some administrative overhead involved in managing these policies.
input_row.reference: 1. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-sas- overview#best-practices-when-using-sas 2. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/storageservices/define-stored-access- policy Additional Information: This recommendation is based on the recommendation Ensure stored access policies (SAP) are used when generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens, from the Common Reference Recommendations > Secrets and Keys > Shared Access Signatures section.

gpt_response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. Navigate to the Azure Portal:
   - Go to the **Storage Accounts** section of the Azure Portal.

2. Select the Storage Account:
   - Choose the specific **storage account** that you wish to inspect.

3. Access Queues:
   - If the resource is a queue, click on **Queues** under the **Data storage** section.

4. Check Stored Access Policies:
   - Click on each **queue name** to go into its settings.
   - Under **Settings**, select **Access policy** and look for **Stored access policies**.

5. Identify Stored Access Policies:
   - Verify if any stored access policies (SAPs) are listed.
   - Check the **Identifier** and **Permissions** to confirm that they conform to your organization's security standards.

6. Examine SAS Tokens:
   - Note that the Azure Portal does not provide a direct list of all SAS tokens. Therefore, for compliance:
     - Check applications and services that utilize SAS tokens and determine how many were generated using SAPs (look for the presence of the **si** parameter in the SAS tokens). 

7. Verify Compliance:
   - Confirm that all SAS tokens are linked to a stored access policy (indicated by the presence of the **si** parameter).
   - If any SAS tokens are found without being linked to a stored access policy, compliance has been compromised.

8. Document Findings:
   - Document any SAS tokens lacking association with stored access policies, along with the necessary details about corrective actions that need to be taken.

**Expected Secure Configuration:**
- All generated SAS tokens should use stored access policies for centralized management.

**How to Confirm Compliance:**
- If the SAS tokens are verified to have an associated stored access policy through applications or services wherever they are utilized, then compliance with the control is achieved. Otherwise, plan to remediate to ensure proper use of stored access policies in future SAS token generation.
